title: Bourbon vs Typica: The Coffee Family Tree ☕💰
description:
The Bourbon and Typica heirloom varietals that descend from a handful of plants smuggled out of Yemen in the 1700s. How two cultivars conquered the global coffee map.

Sources: World Coffee Research, SCA, James Hoffmann, History of coffee research papers, Coffee genetics studies.

segments.voice: Almost every coffee tree on Earth descends from a handful of plants smuggled out of Yemen in the 1700s. Not metaphorically. Genetically. Bourbon and Typica, two heirloom varietals, account for roughly seventy percent of all arabica coffee grown today. Their genetic bottleneck is the reason coffee is so vulnerable to disease.

The story begins in Yemen around the year 1450. Sufi monks in Mocha port roasted and brewed coffee. The bean spread across the Ottoman Empire. By the 1600s, Yemen guarded its coffee monopoly fiercely. Live coffee plants were illegal to export. Only roasted beans could leave the country.

Dutch traders broke the monopoly. In 1616, the Dutch East India Company smuggled live coffee trees out of Mocha. They planted them in their colonies. The first successful planting happened in Java in 1696. From a few smuggled trees, Java coffee was born. That genetic line became Typica.

In 1715, the French planted coffee trees on the island of Bourbon, now known as Reunion, in the Indian Ocean. Those trees came from a different Yemeni population than the Dutch had. The French line became the Bourbon varietal. Two distinct genetic populations from Yemen now seeded two different continents.

Typica spread westward from Java. Dutch traders gave a coffee plant to Louis the Fourteenth in 1714. The plant traveled to Martinique with a French naval officer named Gabriel de Clieu in 1723. From Martinique, Typica colonized the Caribbean, Central America, and South America. Most Latin American coffee today is Typica or its descendants.

Bourbon spread differently. French missionaries took plants from Reunion to East Africa and Brazil in the 1800s. Bourbon proved well-suited to Brazilian conditions. By 1860, Brazil had become the largest coffee producer in the world, mostly growing Bourbon. The varietal became the foundation of Brazilian coffee culture.

How do Bourbon and Typica taste different? Typica produces a clean, balanced cup with mild acidity, medium body, and chocolate notes. Bourbon produces a slightly more complex cup with brighter acidity, fuller body, and red fruit notes. The differences are subtle but real.

Visually, the plants look different. Typica trees grow tall and narrow with bronze-tipped new leaves. Bourbon trees grow shorter and bushier with green-tipped new leaves. Cherries are also different: Typica cherries elongate, Bourbon cherries stay rounder. Farmers can tell them apart at harvest.

Yields differ significantly. Bourbon yields about thirty percent more than Typica per tree. That higher yield drove the Bourbon expansion across Brazil and Africa. Lower yields kept Typica popular for quality but limited its commercial dominance.

Both varietals are vulnerable to coffee leaf rust, called la roya. The fungus, hemileia vastatrix, devastated Sri Lankan and Indonesian Typica plantings in the 1860s and 1870s. Sri Lanka switched to tea entirely. Indonesia switched to robusta. The fungus is still active today, threatening Latin American coffee.

Caturra is a natural mutation of Bourbon discovered in Brazil in 1937. The plant grew shorter, easier to harvest, with higher yields. Caturra became one of the dominant commercial varietals in Latin America. The cup quality matches Bourbon closely but the agronomic gains were huge.

Catuai is a hybrid of Caturra and Mundo Novo, developed in Brazil in the 1950s. It combines compact stature with even higher yields. Catuai dominates Brazilian commercial plantings today. Cup quality is decent but not exceptional. The plant trades flavor for volume.

Mundo Novo, another Brazilian creation, crosses Bourbon and Typica. The hybrid combines high yields from Bourbon with the cup balance of Typica. Mundo Novo was first selected in the 1940s. It became the foundation for many later hybrids, including Catuai.

Pacas is a Bourbon mutation discovered in El Salvador in 1949. Like Caturra in Brazil, Pacas grows shorter while maintaining Bourbon quality. Pacas dominates El Salvador and parts of Honduras. Many specialty coffees from these countries are essentially Bourbon in a compact package.

Pacamara, developed in El Salvador in 1958, crosses Pacas with the elephant bean Maragogype. The cherries are massive, the cup is intense with floral and herbal notes. Pacamara wins specialty competitions regularly. The yields are low but the price commands premiums.

SL28 and SL34 from Kenya descend from Bourbon. Scott Laboratories selected from imported French Mission Bourbon stock in the 1930s. The Bourbon genetics combined with Kenyan altitude produced the famous blackcurrant cup profile. Genetic roots matter.

World Coffee Research published a 2022 study analyzing genetic diversity in arabica. The findings were stark. The entire arabica species shows only one percent of the genetic diversity of robusta. Bourbon and Typica share most of their DNA. The bottleneck happened in Yemen six hundred years ago.

The lack of diversity creates vulnerability. A single disease, single climate shift, or single pest could devastate the global arabica supply. Breeders work urgently to introgress genes from wild coffee species like Coffea racemosa and Coffea liberica into commercial arabica lines.

F1 hybrids represent the future. Crosses between traditional Bourbon-Typica lines and resistant ethiopian wild varieties produce plants with higher yields, disease resistance, and quality cup profiles. Centroamericano and Marsellesa are examples. The F1 hybrids cost more in seed but pay off in resilience.

Brewing Bourbon at home, the cup responds well to medium roast and varied brewing methods. Pour-over highlights the red fruit notes. Espresso emphasizes the body. French press brings out the chocolate. The varietal is forgiving compared to more delicate Geisha or Ethiopian lots.

Brewing Typica at home, the cup is cleaner and lighter. Pour-over methods work best. The Typica cup struggles in espresso because the body is too light. James Hoffmann recommends V60 with ratio one to sixteen, water at ninety-three Celsius, fine-medium grind.

Old Bourbon trees produce better coffee than young ones. The roots dig deeper, accessing minerals that young trees cannot reach. Farms with sixty year old Bourbon plantings, like some in Rwanda and El Salvador, produce cups with unique mineral character. Replanting cycles destroy this slowly.

Yellow Bourbon, a mutation of red Bourbon, produces yellow cherries when ripe. The cup tends slightly sweeter than red Bourbon. Pink Bourbon, a more recent discovery in Colombia, has cherries that ripen to pink. Both mutations command price premiums in specialty markets.

Pink Bourbon has become one of the trendiest varietals in specialty coffee since 2020. Colombian farms in Huila and Tolima sell pink Bourbon lots at thirty to fifty dollars per pound green. The cup tastes like tropical fruit candy with floral aromatics. The hype mostly justifies the price.

But there is something coffee breeders rarely emphasize publicly. The genetic bottleneck means the entire specialty arabica market sits on a knife edge. A single new disease evolved to attack Bourbon-Typica genetics could erase the industry within a decade. The arabica world is fragile.

If you want to explore varietals at home, buy single-origin coffees that name the cultivar. Bourbon from Rwanda or El Salvador. Typica from Jamaica or Indonesia. Pacamara from El Salvador. Pink Bourbon from Colombia. Each one teaches your palate something different about coffee genetics. Next video, Sumatran wet-hulled process and why it tastes like cigars and forest floor. Subscribe.
